skill.name: pwdebug
skill.description: Command-line Playwright debugging for controlling a persistent Chromium browser session, inspecting pages, capturing screenshots, and reviewing console logs.
skill.summary: Operate a reusable Chromium debugging session from the command line.
skill.author: DCjanus

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found command execution, network, filesystem, and script-injection patterns. Most detections are expected for a local Playwright debugging CLI, but the skill still has meaningful risk because it starts Chromium with a CDP port, accepts browser flags, executes caller-provided JavaScript, and stores page data locally. No evidence found of malicious exfiltration, prompt injection, or hidden remote command control.

medium_findings.title: Local Chromium CDP Service with User-Supplied Browser Flags
medium_findings.description: The start command launches Chromium with a local remote debugging port and extends the launch argument list with user-supplied values. The subprocess call uses an argument array instead of a shell, so classic shell injection is not present. The security risk remains because extra browser flags can weaken Chromium protections, and a CDP endpoint gives powerful control over the browser session.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The subprocess launch and user-controlled flag extension are explicit in the code. The endpoint is bound to 127.0.0.1, which limits exposure but does not remove local misuse risk.

medium_findings.title: Caller-Provided JavaScript Executes in the Active Page
medium_findings.description: The evaluate command wraps a user-supplied expression and sends it to Playwright page.evaluate. This is an intended debugging feature, but it can read or modify page data in any connected browser session. It should be used only against trusted pages and sessions.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The expression argument is directly embedded into a function body passed to page.evaluate. This is a confirmed capability with legitimate debugging use and clear misuse potential.

medium_findings.title: Page Text, HTML, and Console Logs Persist Locally
medium_findings.description: The picker can return selected element text and HTML, and the log watcher stores console messages in a cache file. This is useful for debugging but may persist tokens, personal data, or application details if used on sensitive pages.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The data capture and file writes are directly visible. The risk depends on what pages are debugged, so this is medium rather than high.

low_findings.title: Dynamic Import Static Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The flagged imports are normal Python imports from local pwdebug modules. No evidence found that module names come from user input or that arbitrary code is loaded dynamically.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code uses static from-import statements with fixed local module names. This does not match dynamic import behavior.

low_findings.title: Local CDP Network Checks Are Expected
low_findings.description: The hardcoded HTTP URL and IP address point to 127.0.0.1 for Chromium DevTools readiness and connection. No evidence found of outbound data transfer to an external host.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: All confirmed network endpoints are loopback addresses or user-requested navigation targets. This supports legitimate local browser automation behavior.

low_findings.title: Picker Template Triggers Shell and Certificate Heuristics Incorrectly
low_findings.description: The JavaScript picker script contains template literals and keyboard event checks, including e.key. Static analysis interpreted these as shell backticks and key material, but the context is browser UI code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged backticks are inside a returned JavaScript string and are not executed by a shell. The key references are keyboard event fields, not certificate or private key files.

low_findings.title: Process Status Checks Are Limited to Managed Browser State
low_findings.description: The status and stop commands read a saved process ID and use os.kill checks to manage the browser service. This is system introspection, but it is limited to the tool-owned Chromium process state.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The PID is read from the local state file used by the tool. No broader host enumeration or credential discovery was found.

dangerous_patterns.title: Subprocess Launch of Browser Process
dangerous_patterns.description: Chromium is started through subprocess.Popen with a remote debugging port. The call avoids shell execution but still creates a privileged automation surface.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The Popen call is direct and confirmed. The absence of shell=True reduces injection risk, but browser automation remains powerful.

dangerous_patterns.title: Page Evaluation of User Expression
dangerous_patterns.description: The CLI evaluates user-provided JavaScript in the current page. This can expose or mutate page state when used on sensitive applications.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The expression argument flows into page.evaluate. The capability is intentional and clearly security-sensitive.

dangerous_patterns.title: Persistent Local Debug Artifacts
dangerous_patterns.description: Browser service state, console logs, profile data, and screenshots are written to local cache or temporary paths. These files may retain sensitive debugging data.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The file paths and write operations are explicit. Sensitivity depends on debug target content and user handling of generated files.

content.value_statement: Frontend debugging often needs a real browser session that can be reused across commands. This skill provides a CLI for navigation, JavaScript evaluation, screenshots, element picking, and console log review.
actual_capabilities: Starts a persistent Chromium browser service through Playwright.
Navigates the active browser page to a requested URL.
Evaluates JavaScript expressions in the current page.
Captures screenshots to a temporary local file.
Lets users pick page elements and returns selected element details.
Records and tails browser console messages for debugging.
limitations: Only Chromium is supported through a CDP connection.
A local browser service must be started before most commands work.
JavaScript evaluation runs in the active page and can change page state.
Console logs and browser state are stored on the local machine.

use_cases.title: Investigate a Frontend Bug
use_cases.description: Open a page, inspect the title or DOM state, capture a screenshot, and review recent console errors.

use_cases.title: Collect UI Evidence During Review
use_cases.description: Navigate through a browser session and save screenshots or selected element details for bug reports.

use_cases.title: Debug Agent-Driven Web Workflows
use_cases.description: Reuse one browser session while Claude, Codex, or Claude Code checks page state across multiple commands.

prompt_templates.title: Start a Debug Session
prompt_templates.prompt: Start a pwdebug Chromium session, then report the CDP endpoint and current status.

prompt_templates.title: Open and Inspect a Page
prompt_templates.prompt: Use pwdebug to open this URL in a new tab, then evaluate the page title and visible heading text.

prompt_templates.title: Capture Evidence for a Bug
prompt_templates.prompt: Navigate to the failing page, capture a full-page screenshot, and show the most recent console errors.

prompt_templates.title: Debug an Interactive UI State
prompt_templates.prompt: Use pwdebug to inspect the active page, pick the target element, evaluate its state, and summarize likely causes.

output_examples.input: Open a local development page and check whether it rendered.
output_examples.output: The browser opened the requested page. The title and main heading were returned, and no recent console errors were found.

output_examples.input: Capture evidence for a visual layout issue.
output_examples.output: A screenshot path was provided. The selected element details include tag, id, class names, nearby text, and parent structure.

output_examples.input: Review the last 100 browser console messages.
output_examples.output: Recent messages are grouped by time, level, message text, and source location when the browser reports one.

best_practices: Use it on development, staging, or trusted pages whenever possible.
Stop the browser service when debugging is complete.
Review local cache and screenshot files before sharing debug output.
anti_patterns: Do not evaluate JavaScript on pages that contain sensitive user data unless required.
Do not expose the CDP port beyond the local machine.
Do not pass unsafe Chromium flags without understanding their security impact.

faq.question: What does this skill control?
faq.answer: It controls a local Chromium browser through Playwright and the Chrome DevTools Protocol.

faq.question: Does it support browsers other than Chromium?
faq.answer: No. The documented implementation supports Chromium through a CDP connection.

faq.question: Can it run JavaScript in the page?
faq.answer: Yes. The evaluate command runs caller-provided JavaScript in the active page context.

faq.question: Where are logs stored?
faq.answer: Console logs and browser state are stored under the local pwdebug cache directory.

faq.question: Is it safe for production sites?
faq.answer: Use caution. It can read page data, run JavaScript, and persist debug output locally.

faq.question: Why is the security risk medium?
faq.answer: The behavior is legitimate debugging functionality, but browser control and page evaluation are powerful capabilities.
